data.title: IQVIA Holdings Inc.'s (NYSE:IQV) P/E Still Appears To Be Reasonable
data.description:
When close to half the companies in the United States have price-to-earnings ratios (or "P/E's") below 19x, you may consider IQVIA Holdings Inc. (NYSE:IQV) as a stock to avoid entirely with its 32.3x P/E ratio. Nonetheless, we'd need to dig a little deeper to determine if there is a rational basis for the highly elevated P/E.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

IQVIA Holdings hasn't been tracking well recently as its declining earnings compare poorly to other companies, which have seen some growth on average. It might be that many expect the dour earnings performance to recover substantially, which has kept the P/E from collapsing. If not, then existing shareholders may be extremely nervous about the viability of the share price.

View our latest analysis for IQVIA Holdings

Keen to find out how analysts think IQVIA Holdings' future stacks up against the industry? In that case, our free report is a great place to start.
Advertisement

What Are Growth Metrics Telling Us About The High P/E?

IQVIA Holdings' P/E ratio would be typical for a company that's expected to deliver very strong growth, and importantly, perform much better than the market.

If we review the last year of earnings, dishearteningly the company's profits fell to the tune of 4.5%. This has soured the latest three-year period, which nevertheless managed to deliver a decent 20% overall rise in EPS. Although it's been a bumpy ride, it's still fair to say the earnings growth recently has been mostly respectable for the company.

Turning to the outlook, the next three years should generate growth of 15% each year as estimated by the analysts watching the company. Meanwhile, the rest of the market is forecast to only expand by 12% per annum, which is noticeably less attractive.

With this information, we can see why IQVIA Holdings is trading at such a high P/E compared to the market. Apparently shareholders aren't keen to offload something that is potentially eyeing a more prosperous future.

The Final Word

Generally, our preference is to limit the use of the price-to-earnings ratio to establishing what the market thinks about the overall health of a company.

As we suspected, our examination of IQVIA Holdings' analyst forecasts revealed that its superior earnings outlook is contributing to its high P/E. Right now shareholders are comfortable with the P/E as they are quite confident future earnings aren't under threat. Unless these conditions change, they will continue to provide strong support to the share price.

There are also other vital risk factors to consider before investing and we've discovered 1 warning sign for IQVIA Holdings that you should be aware of.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Axon Enterprise (AXON) After Its Strong Multi‑Year Rally?
data.description:

Approach 1: Axon Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a company could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars.

For Axon Enterprise, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about $148 million. Simply Wall St uses analyst estimates out to 2027, where free cash flow is projected at $841.853 million, then extrapolates further, with free cash flow projections between 2026 and 2035 all under $2b and therefore still discussed in millions.

Putting these cash flows together and discounting them back gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $392.06 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around $612.98, the model implies Axon is about 56.4% above this DCF estimate, which points to the shares looking expensive on this specific cash flow view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Axon Enterprise Price vs Sales

For profitable companies that are still heavily reinvesting, P/S can be a useful way to think about value because it compares what you pay for each dollar of revenue without being distorted by short term swings in earnings.

What investors are really doing with a valuation multiple is weighing growth expectations against risk. Higher growth and perceived quality can justify a higher “normal” or “fair” multiple, while higher uncertainty or weaker profitability usually call for a lower one.

Axon Enterprise currently trades on a P/S of 19.04x. That sits well above the Aerospace & Defense industry average of 3.70x and also above the peer average of 8.27x. This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for Axon’s specific earnings growth expectations, profitability, industry and market cap, rather than assuming all companies in the group deserve the same multiple.

On this preferred multiple view, Axon’s current 19.04x P/S sits above the 15.93x Fair Ratio. This points to the shares looking expensive on this metric.

data.title: Lindsay (LNN) Heads Into Q1 2026 With 10.9% Margin And 11.8% EPS Growth
data.description:
Lindsay (LNN) opened its Q1 2026 reporting window with recent quarterly revenue ranging from US$153.6 million to US$187.1 million over fiscal 2025 and basic EPS between US$1.00 and US$2.45, culminating in trailing twelve month EPS of US$6.82 on revenue of US$676.4 million. Over the past five reported quarters, revenue has moved between US$139.2 million and US$187.1 million while quarterly EPS has ranged from US$1.17 to US$2.45. This gives investors a clear view of how earnings track alongside sales through different parts of the cycle and feeds into a trailing net profit margin of 10.9% that frames the quality of these results.

See our full analysis for Lindsay.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the key narratives investors tend to rely on and where those stories might need updating.

See what the community is saying about Lindsay

10.9% margin backed by mid teens earnings growth

  • Over the last 12 months, Lindsay converted US$676.4 million of revenue into US$74.1 million of net income, which works out to a 10.9% net profit margin alongside 11.8% earnings growth and a 12.3% five year average earnings growth rate.
  • Analysts' consensus view ties this steady margin and earnings record to growth drivers such as international irrigation projects and new infrastructure safety products. They also flag that factors such as interest rates in Brazil and the timing of large Road Zipper projects can influence how consistently that 10.9% margin and mid teens earnings growth trend are maintained.
    • Supporters of the consensus point to growth in markets like MENA and Brazil and new product launches as reasons earnings reached about US$76.0 million today, broadly aligned with the 11.8% past year earnings growth and 12.3% five year average.
    • The narrative also highlights that project timing and tariff related costs could affect future operating income, which matters when analysts expect earnings to reach US$86.5 million and margins around 11.5% by 2028.

P/E of 18.1x versus higher industry multiples

  • Lindsay trades on a trailing P/E of 18.1x, which sits below both the US Machinery industry average of 25.6x and the peer average of 22.7x. The current share price of US$126.64 is about 22.4% below an indicated DCF fair value of roughly US$163.28.
  • Supportive investors argue this valuation gap lines up with the earnings profile. Earnings have grown 11.8% over the last year with analysts expecting about 12.8% yearly growth, yet the stock still carries a lower P/E and a discount to the US$163.28 DCF fair value, even after factoring in that forecast revenue growth of 7% a year trails the 10.5% US market revenue pace.
    • Backers of the bullish narrative also highlight that consensus expects earnings of US$86.5 million and EPS of US$7.88 by around 2028. This would imply a 22.4x P/E if the stock traded at the allowed analyst target of US$131.00.
    • What stands out is that this future 22.4x P/E assumption is still below the 24.7x P/E cited for the US Machinery industry, suggesting the current 18.1x P/E and 22.4% discount to the DCF fair value are being weighed against slower 7% revenue growth versus the broader market.
Have a closer look at why some investors see a valuation gap here and how it ties back to Lindsay's earnings profile. 🐂 Lindsay Bull Case

7% revenue growth versus faster US market

  • Revenue growth for Lindsay is cited at 7% per year, below the referenced 10.5% annual revenue growth rate for the broader US market, even as earnings growth is expected around 12.8% per year compared with a 16% US market earnings growth forecast.
  • More cautious investors focus on this gap, arguing that slower 7% top line growth and below market 12.8% forecast earnings growth could limit how far valuation can stretch, especially when parts of the business like North American irrigation are described as not expecting significant near term improvement and when factors such as higher rates in Brazil or tariff related input costs could influence demand and profitability.
    • These bearish points connect directly to the consensus that the timing of large infrastructure projects can affect revenue streams, which matters when the analysts' framework assumes revenues reach US$751.5 million by 2028 to justify their price assumptions.
    • Critics also point to potential tariff impacts on goods from Taiwan and Korea and possible pressure on US farmers from retaliatory tariffs on agricultural exports as specific ways that the reported 7% revenue growth rate could differ from the 10.5% broader US market trend.

data.title: Nippon BS Broadcasting (TSE:9414) EPS Weakness Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:

The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥2,892 million and EPS of ¥20.49 in Q1 2025 to ¥2,879 million and EPS of ¥14.59 in Q1 2026, while trailing twelve month EPS shifted from ¥88.23 to ¥69.60 and revenue from ¥12,179 million to ¥11,799 million, highlighting pressure on both the top line and per share profitability. With trailing net profit margins compressing and multi year earnings drifting lower, the latest numbers land in a context where investors are likely to focus on whether profitability can stabilize from here.

  • Over the last 12 months, Nippon BS Broadcasting earned a trailing net profit margin of 10.5%, down from 12.9% a year earlier, alongside trailing revenue of ¥11,799 million and net income of ¥1,240 million.
  • Bears highlight the 6.2% annual earnings decline over five years as a sign of weaker profitability, and the margin slip from 12.9% to 10.5% supports that concern, yet

TTM EPS Slides To ¥69.60

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to the present using a required rate of return.

For Rubrik, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at about US$237.2 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections used by Simply Wall St extend out ten years, with projected free cash flow of US$1.5b in 2035. In simple terms, higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/S multiple. In contrast, slower expected growth or higher uncertainty usually point to a lower, more conservative P/S level.

Every investor in Challenger Limited (ASX:CGF) should be aware of the most powerful shareholder groups. And the group that holds the biggest piece of the pie are retail investors with 43% ownership. In other words, the group stands to gain the most (or lose the most) from their investment into the company.

Following a 3.7% decrease in the stock price last week, retail investors suffered the most losses, but institutions who own 37% stock also took a hit.

Let's take a closer look to see what the different types of shareholders can tell us about Challenger.

View our latest analysis for Challenger

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Challenger?

Institutions typically measure themselves against a benchmark when reporting to their own investors, so they often become more enthusiastic about a stock once it's included in a major index. We would expect most companies to have some institutions on the register, especially if they are growing.

As you can see, institutional investors have a fair amount of stake in Challenger. This implies the analysts working for those institutions have looked at the stock and they like it. But just like anyone else, they could be wrong. If multiple institutions change their view on a stock at the same time, you could see the share price drop fast. It's therefore worth looking at Challenger's earnings history below. Of course, the future is what really matters.

Hedge funds don't have many shares in Challenger. Our data shows that Dai-ichi Life Holdings, Inc. is the largest shareholder with 20% of shares outstanding. The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. is the second largest shareholder owning 5.5% of common stock, and State Street Global Advisors, Inc. holds about 5.1% of the company stock.

After doing some more digging, we found that the top 12 have the combined ownership of 51% in the company, suggesting that no single shareholder has significant control over the company.

Researching institutional ownership is a good way to gauge and filter a stock's expected performance. The same can be achieved by studying analyst sentiments. There are a reasonable number of analysts covering the stock, so it might be useful to find out their aggregate view on the future.

Insider Ownership Of Challenger

The definition of an insider can differ slightly between different countries, but members of the board of directors always count. The company management answer to the board and the latter should represent the interests of shareholders. Notably, sometimes top-level managers are on the board themselves.

I generally consider insider ownership to be a good thing. However, on some occasions it makes it more difficult for other shareholders to hold the board accountable for decisions.

Our data suggests that insiders own under 1% of Challenger Limited in their own names. It is a pretty big company, so it would be possible for board members to own a meaningful interest in the company, without owning much of a proportional interest. In this case, they own around AU$10m worth of shares (at current prices). It is good to see board members owning shares, but it might be worth checking if those insiders have been buying.

General Public Ownership

The general public, who are usually individual investors, hold a 43% stake in Challenger. This size of ownership, while considerable, may not be enough to change company policy if the decision is not in sync with other large shareholders.

Public Company Ownership

Public companies currently own 20% of Challenger stock. This may be a strategic interest and the two companies may have related business interests. It could be that they have de-merged. This holding is probably worth investigating further.
